column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Shale Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/stone/sedimentary/shale-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Shale surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Shale mineral absorption characteristics
Beam spot diameter for effective Shale cleaning resolution
Optimal repetition rate for continuous Shale surface processing
Fluence threshold for effective Shale material removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient Shale ablation with minimal heat diffusion
Optimal scanning speed for uniform Shale surface treatment
Recommended number of passes for complete Shale surface cleaning
Optimal beam overlap for uniform Shale surface cleaning

Typical range for industrial ns-pulsed fiber lasers at 1064 nm cleaning soft rocks like shale; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and avoids thermal cracking due to shale's low thermal conductivity (~1-2 W/m·K). Max safe before damage ~500 W to prevent substrate ablation. Source: Manufacturer specs (e.g., IPG Photonics YLR series) and studies on laser cleaning of stone (Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

Effective speeds for contamination removal on shale; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal fluence delivery without overheating. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., Laserax systems) and case studies on rock surface preparation.

Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of shale to achieve ablation with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but less common industrially due to cost. Avoid fs for shale to prevent micro-cracking from shock waves. Recommended 30 ns for low reflectivity materials. Source: Laser physics research (Applied Surface Science, 2020) on pulsed laser ablation thresholds.

Typical for efficient cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation in low-conductivity shale, leading to thermal stress. Recommended 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: low freq for heavy contamination to allow cooling. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (Journal of Laser Applications, 2019).

Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging shale (ablation threshold ~2-4 J/cm² for siliceous rocks); below 0.5 J/cm² ineffective for heavy soiling. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning, considering shale's moderate reflectivity (~20-30% at 1064 nm) and melting point (~1200°C). Source: Ablation threshold studies (International Journal of Rock Mechanics, 2017) on laser-rock interactions.

Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine detail on shale surfaces, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area prep. Recommended 200 μm for uniform energy distribution on irregular shale textures. Source: Equipment specifications (e.g., Coherent Armoni series) and process optimization for stone cleaning.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on shale to ensure complete removal without excessive energy input. Recommended 2 passes for industrial surface preparation, allowing incremental cleaning to minimize damage risk. Source: Industry practice and case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of geological samples in Applied Optics, 2021).

Ensures uniform coverage; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for detailed shale cleaning to avoid gaps in irregular surfaces. Recommended 60% for optimal uniformity without overexposure. Source: Process optimization studies (Lasers in Engineering, 2016) on scan strategies for rock substrates.
